questions.source: SRE book introduction; SRE workbook TOC
questions.question: In SRE vocabulary, which definition correctly distinguishes SLI, SLO, and SLA?
options: SLI is a measured indicator; SLO is an internal target for that indicator; SLA is a contractual commitment often with consequences
SLI, SLO, and SLA are interchangeable synonyms for uptime percentage
SLA is always stricter than SLO because legal teams set higher bars
SLI is a marketing term; only SLO matters operationally
questions.explanation: SRE book and workbook establish: SLIs are quantified service aspects (latency, availability); SLOs are target ranges for SLIs; SLAs are external agreements, often looser than internal SLOs. This hierarchy drives error budgets and alerting design.

questions.source: SRE book introduction — error budget chapter
questions.question: Google's error budget model resolves tension between product development and SRE by:
options: Allowing a defined amount of unreliability that can be 'spent' on launches and risk, aligning incentives when budget remains
Requiring 100% availability for all services except pacemakers
Eliminating SRE involvement once a service launches
Making SRE solely responsible for all outages regardless of launch decisions
questions.explanation: SRE book intro: 100% reliability is the wrong target for most services. Error budget = 1 − SLO target; teams spend it on velocity. When budget is exhausted, focus shifts to stability — a structured trade-off SRE-SWE candidates should articulate.

questions.source: SRE book — eliminating toil; workbook TOC
questions.question: Which activity best exemplifies SRE 'toil' that should be reduced over time?
options: Manually restarting the same failing batch job every morning with no lasting fix
Writing automation that permanently eliminates that restart requirement
Designing a new feature's architecture for launch review
Conducting a blameless postmortem after an incident
questions.explanation: SRE defines toil as manual, repetitive, automatable work tied to running a service — not engineering that permanently improves the system. Eliminating toil frees SRE capacity for software-driven reliability improvements.

questions.question: A blameless postmortem after a production incident primarily aims to:
options: Understand systemic causes and prevent recurrence without punishing individuals for honest mistakes
Identify a single engineer to hold accountable for termination
Document incidents only for legal compliance with no process changes
Avoid writing anything down to protect the team from management
questions.explanation: Blameless postmortems are core SRE culture. Focus is systemic fixes, shared learning, and action items — not individual blame. This connects to leadership behavioral prep (growth from failure).

questions.question: Non-Abstract Large System Design (NALSD) differs from abstract system design interviews by emphasizing:
options: Iterative, concrete designs with capacity planning, failure domains, and operational cost — not hand-wavy boxes
Only theoretical CAP theorem proofs without numbers
Coding complete implementations during the interview
Ignoring failure modes until after launch
questions.explanation: NALSD chapter: start with requirements, iterate through designs increasing sophistication until viable, with resource planning and failure defense. SRE-SWE path values this concrete iteration over abstract diagrams — closely aligned with the 60-min Systems Architecture rubric.

questions.question: How does Google SRE differ from traditional operations according to the SRE book?
options: SRE engineers use software and automation to solve operations problems and share ownership of reliability with development
SRE only responds to pages and never writes code
SRE replaces all product developers on every team
Traditional ops and SRE are identical at Google
questions.explanation: SRE book intro positions SRE as software engineers who apply engineering to operations: automation, SLO-driven decisions, shared responsibility with dev teams. The SRE role overview reflects availability, automation, capacity, and postmortems.

questions.question: NALSD iterative design phases are often summarized as moving through:
options: Possible → better → feasible → resilient → better again (with requirements driving each iteration)
Perfect design on first attempt → no changes ever
Code first → document later → ignore requirements
Only resilience matters; feasibility can be deferred indefinitely
questions.explanation: NALSD and QUIZ-PLAN B5 reference this iteration arc: prove a sketch works, refine with numbers, validate feasibility, add resilience, then optimize. Deferring reliability early means fewer features at higher cost later — a key SRE design principle.

questions.question: SRE best practice for alerting aligns with:
options: Paging humans only when SLO-threatening symptoms occur, not on every metric fluctuation
Alerting on every CPU spike above 10% regardless of user impact
Disabling alerts during launches to avoid noise
Emailing the entire company on each minor log warning
questions.explanation: SRE workbook chapters on SLO-based alerting advocate symptom-based, SLO-aligned pages — reducing alert fatigue and tying operational response to user-visible reliability. Connects to B7 detection vs noise.

questions.source: SRE book; NALSD; SRE role overview
questions.question: Which sequence best captures the SRE engagement lifecycle for a new large service?
options: Design review → launch readiness → operate/monitor → iterate/refine reliability
Launch without review → ignore metrics → rewrite from scratch annually
SRE joins only after three years of production pain
Development completes all code before SRE is allowed any input
questions.explanation: SRE lifecycle spans early design partnership (launch reviews), ongoing operation with SLOs, and continuous refinement — not bolt-on ops after launch. NALSD embeds reliability iteration during design, not as an afterthought.

questions.question: If a service has exhausted its error budget mid-quarter, what policy response aligns with SRE principles?
options: Freeze risky launches and focus engineering on reliability until budget recovery
Increase launch velocity to compensate for lost time
Raise the SLO target secretly without telling stakeholders
Ignore the budget because users cannot measure downtime
questions.explanation: Error budget exhaustion triggers reliability focus — a negotiated policy between SRE and product. SRE book describes this as resolving the dev/SRE incentive conflict. Secretly moving goalposts undermines the model.

questions.question: Which is a well-formed SLI for a latency-sensitive API?
options: Proportion of requests completing in under 200 ms over a rolling window
Number of engineers on the team
Total lines of code in the repository
Count of coffee cups consumed in the SRE office
questions.explanation: SLIs must be measurable, user-centric indicators — request latency percentiles, availability ratios, freshness lag. SRE workbook SLO chapters stress choosing SLIs that reflect user experience, then setting SLO targets and error budgets atop them.

questions.question: NALSD states that deferring reliability issues during design is akin to:
options: Accepting fewer features at higher operational cost later
Getting more features faster with no long-term penalty
Eliminating the need for SRE entirely
Improving candidate performance in coding interviews
questions.explanation: NALSD opening argument: reliability is the most critical production feature. Deferring failure defense and capacity planning creates expensive retrofit work — directly relevant to Systems Architecture interviews scoring failure response and iteration.

questions.question: An SRE team spends 70% of time on manual ticket-driven work with no automation roadmap. What principle says this is unsustainable?
options: SRE work should cap toil (commonly ~50% guideline) and invest remaining capacity in engineering that reduces future toil
High toil is ideal because it proves the team is busy
Toil should increase each quarter to justify headcount
Automation is discouraged because it eliminates SRE jobs immediately
questions.explanation: SRE book's toil chapter advocates limiting manual operational load so SREs can engineer durable fixes. Unchecked toil means no time for automation, reliability projects, or design review — undermining the SRE model described in intro and USENIX hiring paper.
